ShortDescription: Process-level spontaneous organization of amphiphiles into bilayers and vesicles that establish primitive boundary geometry and compartmental segregation.
Description: Amphiphile Self-Assembly into Bilayers and Vesicles denotes the admissible process-level arising in which amphiphilic molecules spontaneously organize into bilayer sheets, closed vesicles, or related boundary-bearing structures through hydrophobic effects, packing constraints, curvature tendencies, and interfacial energy minimization. Within Level 3 this arising is not merely the existence of amphiphilic molecules or a finished vesicle object, but the dynamic self-assembly regime through which dispersed components generate stable compartment-forming boundaries in aqueous environments. The arising establishes a protocellular boundary substrate that enables spatial segregation, retention of reactants, controlled permeability, and concentration maintenance while coupling internal chemistry to surrounding gradients. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a process because the ontological unit is the self-organizing boundary-formation regime itself rather than the resulting bilayer or vesicle considered independently. This arising provides the structural basis for primitive compartment geometry, membrane-like enclosure, spatial differentiation of chemical environments, and the emergence of boundary-mediated organization prior to fully developed cellular autonomy.

Evidence.Reference: Experimental formation of vesicles from fatty acids and related amphiphiles, including microscopy and biophysical characterization of bilayers, closure behavior, permeability, and stability.
Thermodynamic, packing-parameter, curvature, and interfacial-energy models of amphiphile self-assembly describing bilayer formation, vesicle closure, and boundary stability regimes.

Name: Selective Permeability in Protocellular Boundaries
ShortDescription: Property-level differential permeability of primitive boundaries that regulates exchange and stabilizes distinct internal chemical conditions.
Description: Selective Permeability in Protocellular Boundaries denotes the admissible property-level arising in which primitive membrane-like boundary structures allow differential passage of molecules and ions, thereby establishing controlled internal chemical conditions without requiring fully developed transport machinery. Within Level 3 this arising is not a single diffusion event or a boundary object considered in isolation, but a functional property of protocellular enclosure through which boundary composition, thickness, packing state, and physicochemical characteristics bias partitioning, retention, and transboundary exchange. The arising establishes a primitive control capability that allows some species to enter or leave more readily than others, enabling retention of polymers and reactive intermediates while still permitting nutrient influx and waste efflux. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a property because the ontological unit is the enduring exchange-selectivity capability of the protocellular boundary rather than the individual transport events through which it is manifested. This arising provides the structural basis for stabilization of distinct internal composition, maintenance of reaction-supporting concentration differences, and support of sustained proto-metabolic organization within bounded chemical systems.

Evidence.Reference: Permeability measurements of fatty-acid vesicles and other primitive membranes under varying ionic strength, pH, and compositional conditions demonstrating differential uptake and retention of molecular species.
Diffusion, partitioning, and boundary-constraint models relating primitive membrane composition, packing state, and electrostatic context to selective transport and retention properties.

Name: Encapsulation of Polymers and Catalysts in Protocells
ShortDescription: Process-level confinement of functional polymers and catalysts within primitive compartments, enabling localized reaction networks and stabilized biochemical coupling.
Description: Encapsulation of Polymers and Catalysts in Protocells denotes the admissible process-level arising in which bounded compartments such as vesicles, coacervate droplets, or mixed protocellular boundary structures capture and retain informational polymers, catalysts, and related macromolecular components. Within Level 3 this arising is not merely the existence of a compartment object or the passive presence of internal molecules, but the dynamic confinement regime through which enclosure, retention, reduced dilution, enhanced proximity, and controlled exchange jointly create localized biochemical microenvironments. The arising establishes a condition in which templates and catalysts become co-localized within bounded internal space, allowing reaction coupling, selective amplification, and network persistence to become more stable than in unconfined media. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a process because the ontological unit is the confinement-and-retention regime itself rather than the protocell object or the encapsulated molecules considered independently. This arising provides the structural basis for localized reaction intensification, persistence of functional polymers, early genotype-phenotype coupling, and the emergence of compartment-supported biochemical organization.

Evidence.Reference: Encapsulation experiments using vesicles, coacervates, and related protocellular compartments containing RNA, peptides, catalysts, and other macromolecular components.
Reaction-diffusion, stochastic, and compartmental models demonstrating how confinement enhances local concentration, network persistence, catalytic coupling, and template-catalyst co-stabilization.

Name: Coupled Growth–Division Cycles of Protocellular Compartments
ShortDescription: Hypothesized process-level coupling of protocellular growth and division-like recurrence through material uptake, boundary expansion, and mechanically or chemically induced fission.
Description: Coupled Growth–Division Cycles of Protocellular Compartments denote a hypothesized process-level regime in which primitive compartments undergo recurrent expansion through material uptake and internal synthesis, followed by division through mechanical instability, curvature stress, or environmentally induced fission. Within Level 3 this arising is not merely the existence of vesicles or isolated fission events, but a coupled life-cycle regime through which boundary growth, internal chemical activity, resource access, and instability thresholds become dynamically linked. The arising establishes a reproducible compartmental cycle in which expansion and partitioning are not fully random but are conditioned by material availability, membrane state, and internal composition. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a process because the ontological unit is the recurrent growth-division regime itself rather than the compartment object or the inherited contents considered independently. This arising provides the structural basis for non-random inheritance of internal constituents, sustained protocellular populations, and the emergence of preselection dynamics at the level of bounded chemical compartments.

Evidence.Reference: Experimental observations of fatty-acid vesicle growth and division under controlled feeding, shear stress, osmotic perturbations, and related protocellular boundary-expansion conditions.
Mechanical and thermodynamic models of vesicle growth, curvature-driven instability, fission thresholds, and coupling between material influx, membrane expansion, and compartment division.

Name: Chemiosmotic Energy Transduction in Proto-Membranes
ShortDescription: Property-level use of electrochemical gradients across primitive boundaries to bias internal reactions and sustain non-equilibrium energetic coupling.
Description: Chemiosmotic Energy Transduction in Proto-Membranes denotes the admissible property-level arising in which electrochemical gradients across primitive boundary structures become usable sources of free-energy coupling for internal biochemical organization. Within Level 3 this arising is not a single gradient-dissipation event or a membrane object alone, but a protocellular energetic property through which proton or ion gradients act as persistent non-equilibrium constraints that bias reaction directions, support coupling of otherwise unfavorable steps, and stabilize network operation prior to fully developed respiratory or transport machinery. The arising establishes a directional energetic relation between boundary-maintained gradients and interior chemical transformation, allowing boundary properties to participate directly in the regulation of internal biochemical dynamics. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a property because the ontological unit is the enduring gradient-coupling capability of the proto-membrane system rather than the individual flux events through which it is expressed. This arising provides the structural basis for primitive chemiosmotic biasing, non-equilibrium energetic support of internal reaction networks, and early boundary-mediated energy transduction in protocellular organization.

Evidence.Reference: Non-equilibrium thermodynamics and proto-chemiosmotic models linking electrochemical gradients across primitive boundaries to reaction biasing and early energetic coupling.

Experimental demonstrations of gradient formation and limited energetic coupling in model membranes and protocell systems, including measurable reaction bias contingent on maintained boundary gradients.

Name: Protocellular Selection as a Population-Level Effect
ShortDescription: Effect-level differential persistence and frequency change among protocellular compartments arising from variation in chemistry, stability, and growth-division performance.
Description: Protocellular Selection as a Population-Level Effect denotes the admissible effect-level arising in which heterogeneous protocellular compartments exhibit differential persistence, growth, and division-like recurrence under shared environmental constraints, causing some compartment types to increase in relative frequency over time. Within Level 3 this arising is not a single protocell object or one internal chemical process, but an ensemble-level outcome generated when differences in boundary maintenance, internal catalytic organization, encapsulated composition, and resource coupling are filtered across a population. The arising establishes a transition from isolated compartmental chemistry to population-level configurational sorting, where physicochemical performance differentials produce systematic shifts in distribution even in the absence of fully developed genetic inheritance. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as an effect because the ontological unit is the aggregate population-level consequence of many interacting protocellular trajectories rather than any individual compartment or specific local mechanism. This arising provides the structural basis for early selection-like dynamics, enrichment of more persistent compartment types, and the emergence of ensemble-level filtering prior to mature biological evolutionary regimes.

Evidence.Reference: Minimal selection and protocell ensemble frameworks linking compartment stability, growth-division recurrence, and internal reaction-network efficiency to differential persistence and frequency change.

Agent-based and reaction-diffusion simulations demonstrating differential persistence, enrichment, and frequency shifts among protocellular populations with heterogeneous internal chemistry and boundary stability.

Name: Photopigment Photon Absorption and Isomerization
ShortDescription: Process-level primary photochemical conversion of absorbed photon energy into a localized chemical state change in a photopigment molecule.
Description: Photopigment Photon Absorption and Isomerization denotes the admissible process-level arising in which a photopigment molecule absorbs a photon and undergoes a rapid quantized conformational change that converts electromagnetic input into a discrete chemical state difference. Within Level 3 this arising is not merely the existence of a photopigment object, but the initiating photochemical transduction regime through which an embedded chromophore enters an excited state and proceeds through ultrafast isomerization within a structured protein environment. The arising establishes the first biochemical signal-bearing state change that can be amplified, regulated, and integrated by downstream biochemical networks. Within SOMA this phenomenon is classified as a process because the ontological unit is the photon-triggered conversion event itself rather than the photopigment molecule considered independently. This arising provides the structural basis for phototransduction onset and marks the boundary at which physical photon interaction becomes chemically usable biochemical signaling without presupposing higher sensory or neural organization.

Evidence.Reference: Photochemistry of retinal chromophores, excited-state dynamics models, and quantum descriptions of ultrafast photoisomerization in protein-constrained pigment systems.

Ultrafast spectroscopy, crystallographic trapping of photointermediates, and mutational studies of photopigments demonstrating single-photon-triggered chromophore isomerization and reproducible early-state transitions.
